sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 7 and Ch. 8 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Ch. 8 Q1: the Solutions Guide prints the answer as 'short-term memory' but the live 2e option C reads 'working memory' (Baddeley's model); the keyed letter C is unchanged and 'working memory' is the accurate term.

questions.stem: Cognitive psychology is the branch of psychology that focuses on the study of ________.
questions.explanation: Cognitive psychology is the field that studies human thinking, including perception, language, attention, problem solving, and memory.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q1 = B.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of an artificial concept?
questions.explanation: An artificial concept is defined by a specific set of rules or features. A triangle's area (1/2 base x height) is defined by a precise mathematical rule, making it an artificial concept; natural concepts like mammals develop from experience.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q3 = B.

questions.stem: ________ provides general principles for organizing words into meaningful sentences.
questions.explanation: Syntax refers to the rules and principles for organizing words into grammatically correct, meaningful sentences.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q5 = D.

questions.stem: The meaning of words and phrases is determined by applying the rules of ________.
questions.explanation: Semantics is the set of rules governing the meaning of words and phrases in a language.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q7 = D.

questions.stem: A specific formula for solving a problem is called ________.
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a specific, step-by-step formula or procedure that, if followed correctly, guarantees a solution to a problem.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q9 = A.

questions.stem: Which type of bias involves becoming fixated on a single trait of a problem?
questions.explanation: Anchoring bias is the tendency to focus on (anchor to) one particular piece of information or trait when making decisions.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q11 = A.

choices.label: B
choices.text: being able to create new products

choices.label: C
choices.text: being able to understand and communicate with different cultures

choices.label: D
choices.text: being able to see complex relationships and solve problems

questions.explanation: Fluid intelligence is the capacity to reason, see complex relationships, and solve novel problems, independent of acquired knowledge (crystallized intelligence).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q13 = D.

questions.stem: Which theorist put forth the triarchic theory of intelligence?
questions.explanation: Robert Sternberg proposed the triarchic theory of intelligence, comprising practical, creative, and analytical intelligence.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q15 = C.

questions.stem: In order for a test to be normed and standardized it must be tested on ________.
questions.explanation: Norming and standardizing a test requires administering it to a representative sample of the population so that scores can be meaningfully compared.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q17 = B.

questions.stem: Who developed the IQ test most widely used today?
questions.explanation: David Wechsler developed the Wechsler scales (e.g., the WAIS), the most widely used individual IQ tests today.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q19 = D.

questions.stem: Where does high intelligence come from?
questions.explanation: Intelligence arises from an interaction of both genetics and environment; neither alone fully accounts for it.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q21 = C.

questions.explanation: Learning disabilities are neurological disorders that affect specific cognitive processes (such as reading or math) and are distinct from overall intelligence.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 7 Q23 = B.

questions.stem: ________ is a memory store with a phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, and a central executive.
questions.explanation: Baddeley and Hitch's model of working memory comprises the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, and central executive.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from the live OpenStax page (option C = 'working memory'). The Student Solutions Guide prints the Ch. 8 Q1 answer as 'short-term memory'; the keyed letter C is unchanged and 'working memory' is the correct term for Baddeley's model.

questions.stem: The three functions of memory are ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: automatic processing, effortful processing, and storage

choices.label: C
choices.text: automatic processing, effortful processing, and retrieval

questions.explanation: Memory has three core functions: encoding (getting information in), storage (retaining it), and retrieval (getting it back out).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q3 = D.

questions.stem: An exceptionally clear recollection of an important event is a (an) ________.
questions.explanation: A flashbulb memory is an exceptionally vivid, detailed 'snapshot' memory of an emotionally significant or surprising event.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q5 = C.

questions.stem: Tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is also known as ________.
questions.explanation: The tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is a form of blocking: the temporary inability to retrieve information that you know is stored in memory.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q7 = D.

questions.stem: When you are learning how to play the piano, the statement "Every good boy does fine" can help you remember the notes E, G, B, D, and F for the lines of the treble clef. This is an example of a (an) ________.
questions.explanation: An acrostic is a mnemonic in which the first letter of each word in a phrase cues the items to be remembered (here, E-G-B-D-F).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q9 = C.

choices.label: A
choices.text: making the material you are trying to memorize personally meaningful to you

choices.label: B
choices.text: making a phrase of all the first letters of the words you are trying to memorize

choices.label: C
choices.text: making a word formed by the first letter of each of the words you are trying to memorize

choices.label: D
choices.text: saying words you want to remember out loud to yourself

questions.explanation: The self-referencing effect is the tendency to remember information better when you relate it to yourself, making the material personally meaningful.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 8 Q11 = A.
